1.1: epeideper polloi epecheirésan anatasasthai diegésin — since many have undertaken to compile a narrative — Luke's prologue is the most literary sentence in the NT, using classical Greek historical prologues style (cf. Thucydides, Josephus). Epeideper (since indeed) is formal; anatasasthai diegesin (to draw up a narrative) uses technical historiographical vocabulary. anothen (from the beginning, from above) — Luke claims to have investigated everything carefully (parakolouthekoti, perfect participle: having followed alongside, investigated thoroughly) and writes in order (kathexes) for Theophilus to know the certainty (asphaleia) of the things taught.
1.46: megalynei he psyche mou ton kyrion — my soul magnifies the Lord — the Magnificat is a mosaic of OT texts, especially Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:1-10). Megalynei (magnifies, declares great) is the same verb as LXX Ps 34:3 (megazynen ton kyrion). he tapeinosis tes doules autou — the humble estate of his servant — tapeinosis (lowliness, humility) is the covenant-quality of the anawim; Mary identifies herself with the humble poor of Israel who trust YHWH's reversals. The seven reversals that follow (vv.51-53) are the Magnificat's theological core: each pair presents the divine inversion of human expectation.

2.11: soter hos estin Christos Kyrios — a Savior who is Christ the Lord — the angelic birth announcement stacks three titles on the newborn: soter (savior — the most frequent soteriological title in Luke-Acts, used 4x in Luke, rarely in Matthew/Mark), Christos (the Messiah), and Kyrios (Lord — the LXX name for YHWH). The combination of all three titles in one announcement is Luke's programmatic Christological declaration. en polei David — in the city of David — the Davidic geography grounds the birth in covenant geography.
2.29: nyn apolueis ton doulon sou Despota — now you are releasing your servant, Master, in peace — the Nunc Dimittis of Simeon is structured as a release-prayer: apolueis (you release, let go) uses the legal-release term for a slave being freed or a prisoner being discharged; Simeon describes his death as a release from watchful-keeping-duty, now that he has seen the salvation. Despota (Master, lord with absolute authority) is the prayer address; eirene (peace) is the shalom of complete wellbeing at death's threshold.

3.22: sy ei ho huios mou ho agapetos, en soi eudokesa — you are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased — Luke's baptism declaration is identical to Mark's; a variant reading (D manuscript) gives you are my Son, today I have begotten you, quoting Ps 2:7 directly. The standard text combines Ps 2:7 (my Son) with Gen 22:2 (agapetos = only/beloved son) and Isa 42:1 (with you I am well pleased = my chosen one in whom my soul delights). The triplicity of the title signals the depth of the Christological identification.

4.18: pneuma kyriou ep eme hou heneken echrisen me — the Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me — the Nazareth synagogue reading is the programmatic Christological text for Luke's Gospel. Echrisen (anointed) shares the root with Christos; Jesus identifies himself as the Spirit-anointed one of Isa 61. The four infinitives that follow specify the anointing's purpose: euangelisasthai ptochois (proclaim good news to the poor), keruxai aichmalotos aphesin (announce release to captives), aposteilai tethrausmenous en aphesei (send away the broken in release), keruxai eniauton kyriou dekton (announce the year of the Lord's favor = the Jubilee).
4.21: semeron peplerothe he graphe haute en tois osin hymon — today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing — the adverb semeron (today) is Luke's characteristic eschatological-present marker (also 19:9 salvation came today; 23:43 today you will be with me in paradise). The fulfillment is not a future event Jesus predicts but a present reality he declares happening as he speaks; the scripture's fulfillment is coterminous with his proclamation.

6.20: makarioi hoi ptochoi, hoti hymetera estin he basileia tou theou — blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God — Luke's beatitudes are in the second person (you, not they as in Matthew) and unqualified (poor without spirit; hungry without for righteousness). Luke's social realism presents poverty and hunger as the literal conditions of Jesus's audience; the kingdom reversal is specifically material as well as spiritual. The four beatitudes are balanced by four corresponding woes (6:24-26), creating the sharpest contrast structure in the Synoptic tradition.

7.34: elelythen ho huios tou anthropou esthion kai pinon — the Son of Man has come eating and drinking — the Son of Man self-reference in the social context of table-fellowship is Luke's Christological signature: the one with Danielic universal authority is present at dinner with sinners and tax collectors. The Pharisees' critique (phagos kai oinopotes, philos telonon kai hamartolon = a glutton and a drunkard, friend of tax collectors and sinners) is the social observation; Jesus turns it into the theological point: Wisdom is justified by all her children (v.35).

9.51: en de to sympleroysthai tas hemeras tes analepseos autou — when the days for his assumption were being fulfilled, he set his face to go to Jerusalem — analempsis (assumption, taking up) is a rare word used for Elijah's being taken up (2 Kgs 2:11 in Josephus) and for Jesus's ascension (Acts 1:11). Luke frames the Jerusalem journey (9:51-19:44) as a journey toward the assumption-through-death-resurrection-ascension; Jerusalem is the city where prophets die (13:33) and where the mission is fulfilled. Estersen to prosopon autou (set his face) echoes Isa 50:7 (the Servant who set his face like flint).

10.18: etheoroun ton Satanan hos astrapen ek tou ouranou pesonta — I was watching Satan fall like lightning from heaven — the aorist pesonta (having fallen) suggests a definitive past event rather than a process; the 70's mission success is the visible sign of an already-accomplished heavenly event. Astrapen (lightning) evokes the sudden, complete, and irresistible nature of the fall. The statement is Jesus's interpretation of the exorcism-mission: what the disciples experience as individual deliverances is the symptom of a decisive cosmic defeat of the enemy already accomplished through Jesus's authority.
10.21: exomologoumai soi pater kyrie tou ouranou kai tes ges, hoti apekrupsas tauta apo sophon kai synetōn kai apekalypsas auta nepiois — I thank you Father Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little ones — the prayer is Luke's unique Trinitarian pericope: Jesus addresses the Father, the Spirit's enabling is implied by the eschatological joy (egalliasthato en to pneumati to hagio, v.21), and Jesus speaks as the Son who alone knows the Father (v.22). Nepiois (little children, infants, the simple) are the recipients of revelation because divine grace rather than human capacity is its source.

15.20: heti de autou makran apechontos eiden auton ho pater autou kai esplanchnisthe — while he was still a long way off his father saw him and was moved with compassion — esplanchnisthe is the gut-compassion verb (cf. Luke 7:13 for the widow's son; 10:33 for the Good Samaritan); it is the visceral response that precedes and motivates the action. The sequence in v.20 is rapid and movement-intensive: ran (edrame), fell on his neck (epepesen epi ton tracheton autou), kissed him (katefilesen auton) — three verbs of urgent, excessive welcome that exceed what Mediterranean honor-culture would expect of a father toward a disgraced son. The parable's God-image is defined by this excess of welcoming love.

17.21: he basileia tou theou entos hymon estin — the kingdom of God is in your midst / within you — entos is a rare preposition meaning either within (internal, in your hearts) or in your midst (among you, present in your community). The context (the Pharisees asking when the kingdom comes) favors in your midst: the kingdom is not a future observable event coming at a detectable moment but a present reality already among the Pharisees in the person of Jesus and his ministry. Luke's eschatology is simultaneously present (the kingdom is here) and future (the Son of Man's coming, vv.22-37).

18.13: ho de telones makrothen hestōs ouk ethelen oude tous ophthalmous eis ton ouranon eparai, alla etypton to stethos autou legon ho theos hilastheti moi to hamarolio — the tax collector standing far off would not even lift his eyes to heaven but beat his breast saying God be merciful to me the sinner — hilastheti (be propitious/merciful to me, the aorist passive of hilaskomai) is the LXX atonement verb (used for the priest making atonement on Yom Kippur, Lev 16 LXX). The tax collector prays the Day of Atonement prayer in his own person: God, let atonement be made for me. The article (the sinner, not a sinner) suggests the man sees himself as the paradigm case of sinfulness.

19.10: elthen gar ho huios tou anthropou zetesai kai sosai to apololes — for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost — Luke's mission-statement (parallel to Matt 20:28 / Mark 10:45 but using sozo and apollymi instead of the ransom-language). Zetesai (to seek) echoes the seeking-shepherd of Ezek 34:11 and the seeking-woman of Luke 15:8; sosai (to save) is Luke's characteristic salvation-vocabulary; to apololes (the lost thing, the perishing one) echoes all three parables of Luke 15 (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son). Zacchaeus is the concrete instance of the Son of Man's seeking-and-saving mission.

22.37: kai gar to peri emou telos echei — for what is written about me has its fulfillment — Luke's unique passion-eve citation of Isa 53:12 (he was numbered with the transgressors) is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus explicitly quotes a Servant Song as being about himself and being fulfilled in the impending events. Telos echei (has its end/completion) uses the teleological vocabulary of purpose reaching its goal; the Servant vocation that Jesus has lived throughout the Gospel is now reaching its culminating moment in the passion.

23.46: pater, eis cheiras sou paratithemi to pneuma mou — Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — Luke's uniquely reported last word from the cross quotes Ps 31:5 (the evening prayer of Jewish piety, recited before sleep as a commendation of the soul to God's keeping). The address Pater (Father) is distinctively Lukan (not in Mark's dereliction-cry; Matthew adds the Ps 22 form); even at death Jesus addresses God as Father, maintaining the intimacy of the Gethsemane prayer (22:42). The death is a trust-filled commendation rather than a defeat.

24.27: arxamenos apo Mouseos kai apo panton ton propheton diermeneuen autois en pasais tais graphais ta peri heautou — beginning from Moses and all the prophets he interpreted to them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself — the risen Christ's Emmaus hermeneutic is Luke's programmatic claim about Scripture: all of it (en pasais tais graphais = in all the scriptures) is about him. The verb diermeneuen (he interpreted, explained) is the technical term for authoritative interpretation; Jesus is his own supreme interpreter. The burning hearts (v.32) indicate that the hermeneutic is not merely cognitive but spiritually transformative: right reading of the Scriptures produces encounter with the risen Christ.
24.44: dei plerothenal panta ta gegrammena en to nomo Mouseos kai tois prophetais kai psalmois peri emou — it is necessary for everything written in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms about me to be fulfilled — Luke's risen-Christ statement is the most comprehensive canonical claim in the Gospels: the tripartite Hebrew canon (Torah + Nevi'im + Ketuvim = the Psalms as the Writings' representative) is entirely oriented toward and fulfilled in Jesus. Dei (it is necessary) indicates divine necessity: not merely that these things happened to correspond with Scripture, but that Scripture's fulfillment was divinely required and superintended. The resurrection is the key that unlocks what was always there.
